why: Guards the fallback where a nested/correlated subquery's column reference cannot be resolved to any enclosing table (e.g. a scalar subquery like SELECT (SELECT t1.col) FROM t1), forcing declared type to NULL instead of the column's real type -- easy to craft via a nested correlated scalar subquery.

Only taken when the expression whose affinity is being computed is a TK_VECTOR row-value constructor like (a,b), recursing into its first element -- flip it with a row-value expression, e.g. CAST((1,2) AS TEXT) or a vector comparison.

Diverts comparison codegen to the generic default_expr path whenever the left operand of <,<=,>,>=,=,<>,IS is itself a vector/row-value constructor, e.g. WHERE (1,2)=(1,2) -- straightforward to craft a row-value comparison used as a boolean condition.

Backs the EP_Commuted check in sqlite3ExprCompareCollSeq that decides operand order when picking a comparison's collating sequence; never observed a comparison whose operands the optimizer swapped -- craft a WHERE clause like '5=col COLLATE nocase' that triggers operand-swap optimization to exercise the reversed-order lookup.

Guards a GLOB/LIKE pattern where the escape (or '[') character is the last character immediately after a wildcard '*'/'%', leaving nothing to escape -- craft e.g. "'%x' LIKE '%\' ESCAPE '\'" style trailing-escape pattern to flip it.

Case-insensitive LIKE character comparison (lower(c)==lower(c2) for ASCII) has only ever succeeded, never failed-through to the next check -- craft a LIKE pattern with mismatched letters/case or non-ASCII characters against the string to force this comparison false.

Validates that a window function's frame-boundary combination is legally ordered, rejecting things like 'ROWS BETWEEN CURRENT ROW AND 1 PRECEDING' -- trivial to craft an OVER(...) clause with an out-of-order frame spec to hit the error path.
Disqualifies a FROM-clause term from matching a WITH-clause CTE name whenever it is schema-qualified (e.g. main.cte_name) -- craft 'WITH x AS (...) SELECT * FROM main.x' to force this rejection path.

Requires the built-in match/glob/like/regexp functions to have exactly 2 list arguments when invoked directly as function calls rather than infix operators -- craft 'SELECT match(1)' or 'SELECT like(1,2,3)' with wrong arity to flip it.
